metadata.author: Jonah
metadata.placeOfWriting: In the Northern Kingdom of Israel
metadata.subject: Jehovah’s Salvation Reaching Even to the Gentile City of Nineveh through the Commissioned Prophet.

1: Now the word of Jehovah came to Jonah the son of Amittai, saying,
Arise, go to Nineveh, the great city, and cry out against it, for their wickedness has come up before Me.

But Jonah rose up to flee to Tarshish from the presence of Jehovah. And he went down to Joppa and found a ship going to Tarshish; so he paid the fare for it and went down into it to go with them to Tarshish from the presence of Jehovah.
And Jehovah prepared a great fish to swallow Jonah; and Jonah was in the stomach of the fish three days and three nights.
2: Then Jonah prayed to Jehovah his God from the stomach of the fish.
And he said, I called out of my distress to Jehovah, and He answered me; out of the belly of Sheol I cried; You heard my voice.
3: And the word of Jehovah came to Jonah a second time, saying, Arise, go to Nineveh, the great city, and proclaim to it the proclamation that I tell you.
And the people of Nineveh believed God; and they proclaimed a fast and put on sackcloth, from the greatest of them even to the least of them.
4: But it displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he became angry.

And Jehovah said, You have had pity on the plant, for which you did not labor... And should I not have pity on Nineveh, the great city, in which there are more than a hundred and twenty thousand persons who do not know their right hand from their left?

17.text: The experience of Jonah in the belly of the fish is a sign of Christ’s death and resurrection, as mentioned by the Lord Himself in the Gospels (Matt. 12:40).

9.text: This is the central theme of the book: salvation does not belong to any one race or merit, but is entirely of the Lord's mercy.

11.text: The book ends abruptly to emphasize God's wide-reaching compassion for all humanity, contrasting it with the narrow-mindedness of the prophet.
